macro_segments.title: Introduction and Review of Onaat Mamon and Onaat Devarim

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and dedication for protection of soldiers and cessation of antisemitism
Review of previous learning on overcharging and afflicting with words

macro_segments.title: Prohibition on Mixing Produce from Different Fields

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: prohibition against mixing fruit from different fields even if same quality
Exception for wine: mixing harsh with soft wine improves quality
Prohibition on mixing lees of wine with wine; allowing retention of lees from same wine

macro_segments.title: Rules for Selling Diluted Wine and Restrictions on Middlemen

micro_segments.title: Prohibition on selling diluted wine in stores without informing customer
Cannot sell diluted wine to middlemen (tager) even with disclosure due to temptation to deceive
When dilution is standard practice, it is permitted
Middleman can mix grain and wine from multiple sources
Middleman mixing allowed only if not intentionally deceiving

macro_segments.title: Gemara Discussion of Grain Quality and Wine Mixing Details

micro_segments.title: Baraita: even if new grain is more expensive, cannot mix with old grain because buyers want old grain specifically
Wine mixing only permitted between pressing times when flavors blend
Modern practice allows mixing wine outside pressing time because people are aware and taste before buying
Prohibition on mixing lees from different wines but allowing lees within same wine
Explanation of Rav Yehuda: cannot mix yesterday's wine with today's wine

macro_segments.title: Case of Rava Returning Diluted Wine and the Problem of Selling to Retailers

micro_segments.title: Rava brings wine from store, dilutes it, tastes and dislikes, wants to return diluted wine
Abaye objects: cannot sell diluted wine to retailer even with disclosure
Rava's response: his dilution is so heavy it is obviously known, creating no temptation to deceive

macro_segments.title: Standard Market Practices for Wine Dilution

micro_segments.title: Where it is practice to dilute wine, dilution is permitted with standard ratios
Rav holds mixing wine allowed only during pressing time; modern practice differs

macro_segments.title: Mishnah on Fair Competition: Giving Treats to Children, Price Undercutting, and Beautification

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehuda: storekeeper cannot give roasted grain and nuts to children as unfair competition
Sages permit giving treats because competitors can offer their own treats
Rabbi Yehuda: cannot sell produce below market rate as unfair to other storekeepers
Sages say price reduction is praiseworthy for widening market and benefiting consumers
Abba Shaul: cannot sift beans and sell sifted ones at higher price for labor value
Sages permit sifting when quality is visible and buyer knowingly pays for convenience
Cannot display only top layer of sifted beans with unshelled ones below as deceptive
Prohibition on beautifying person, animal, or object to misrepresent condition

macro_segments.title: Gemara Explanations of Beautification Prohibitions and Permitted Enhancements

micro_segments.title: Rabbis permit treats to children because competitor can offer treats in return
Price reduction should be praised for widening the market rate
Baraita: cannot stiffen animal hair, blow up inner organs, or soak meat in water
Term 'mesharvitin' means making look ruddier or healthier using bran water or combing
Shmuel permits placing fringes on garment because adds beauty without misrepresenting newness
Rav Yehuda permits cleaning colored garments; Rabbah permits tapering linen clothes
Rava and Rav Pappa permit drawing arrows, ladders, or basket designs on garments
Beautification distinctions: old items misrepresented is prohibited; new items enhanced is permitted
Case of old slave dying hair black to appear young; Rava refuses to buy
Slave washes out dye to reveal true age; Rava cites righteous escape from affliction

macro_segments.title: Conclusion of Ona'ah Section and Introduction to Neshech and Tarbit

micro_segments.title: End of ona'ah discussion; introduction to neshech and tarbit (interest prohibitions)
Neshech means taking a bite; Tarbit means taking profit

macro_segments.title: Mishnah Defining Neshech and Tarbit with Examples

micro_segments.title: Neshech example: lending four units of value for five units repayment in money or grain
Tarbit example: purchasing grain at market rate with payment due later at higher price
Grain purchased at market rate to be delivered later is permitted despite price increase
Complication: grain price rises and buyer requests different commodity in exchange

macro_segments.title: Gemara Analysis: Neshech and Tarbit as Single Prohibition from Two Perspectives

micro_segments.title: Gemara infers from Mishnah structure that Neshech and Tarbit are one Torah category
Analysis: cannot have Neshech without Tarbit or vice versa; always two sides of same transaction
Example of currency devaluation: borrower loses, lender gains equally; cannot separate effects
Whether looking at initial value or final value, Neshech and Tarbit always coexist

macro_segments.title: Torah Sources for Neshech and Tarbit in Money and Food

micro_segments.title: Torah explicitly mentions Neshech by money and Tarbit by food; Neshech by food derived
Tarbit by money derived from redundant mention of Neshech to teach both apply everywhere
Conclusion: both Neshech and Tarbit prohibit identical conduct in money and food
